items.question: In the bias–variance tradeoff, model complexity primarily acts as:
options: The knob that trades lower bias for higher variance as capacity increases
A measure of label noise in the dataset
The same thing as demographic fairness bias
A fixed property that does not depend on hyperparameters
items.answer: The knob that trades lower bias for higher variance as capacity increases
items.explanation: Increasing complexity typically reduces systematic error (bias) while increasing sensitivity to the training sample (variance).

items.question: Validation error vs model complexity often looks like:
options: A flat line — complexity does not matter
A U-shape — high at low and high complexity, minimum in between
Always decreasing — more complex is always better
Always increasing — simpler is always better
items.answer: A U-shape — high at low and high complexity, minimum in between
items.explanation: Underfitting (high bias) at low complexity and overfitting (high variance) at high complexity produce a validation minimum in the middle.

items.question: Both training and validation error are high. The best next step is usually to:
options: Decrease model complexity or add stronger regularization
Increase model complexity or add expressive features
Remove half the training data
Ignore validation and minimize training loss only
items.answer: Increase model complexity or add expressive features
items.explanation: High train and val error indicates underfitting (high bias) — the model needs more capacity, not less.

items.question: Training error is very low but validation error is much higher. You should:
options: Increase complexity further to improve validation
Reduce complexity, add regularization, or gather more data
Report training error as the production metric
Remove the validation set to avoid the gap
items.answer: Reduce complexity, add regularization, or gather more data
items.explanation: Low train with high val is overfitting (high variance) — complexity is likely too high for the sample.

items.question: Which is a direct complexity knob for decision trees?
options: max_depth or minimum samples per leaf
Learning rate of the optimizer only
Batch size during inference
Number of CSV columns in the raw file
items.answer: max_depth or minimum samples per leaf
items.explanation: Tree depth and leaf size control how many splits and how specific rules can be — classic capacity controls.

items.question: When selecting among complexity settings, you should prefer:
options: The setting with lowest training error
The simplest model whose validation error is near the minimum
The model with the most parameters regardless of validation
The highest complexity that runs on GPU
items.answer: The simplest model whose validation error is near the minimum
items.explanation: Validation (or CV) error estimates generalization; Occam-style selection avoids unnecessary variance and cost.
